question_text: Which of Dorsey's moat types best describes this company's competitive advantage?

options.label: Intangible asset — brand, patent, or regulatory license
Switching cost — customers are locked in by cost of changing
Network effect — product becomes more valuable with each user
Cost advantage — structurally lower costs than competitors
Multiple moats — two or more of the above reinforce each other
None identified — no clear structural advantage

why_this_matters: Pat Dorsey's moat taxonomy (from 'The Little Book That Builds Wealth') provides the clearest framework for classifying competitive advantages. Each moat type has different durability characteristics: network effects tend to be the most durable but also the most fragile if they unravel; intangible assets vary widely (patents expire, brands rarely do); switching costs are reliable but can be disrupted by technology. Identifying the moat type tells you what to monitor.

worked_examples.situation: Merchants accept Visa because consumers carry it; consumers carry Visa because merchants accept it. Each new participant makes the network more valuable for all others.
worked_examples.outcome: Network effects give Visa 65%+ gross margins with minimal competitive pressure. The moat deepens with every transaction processed.

worked_examples.situation: Produces commodity items with no brand recognition, no patents, interchangeable customers, and costs in line with competitors.
worked_examples.outcome: Without a moat, the company earns its cost of capital at best. Margins will compress to industry average as competitors enter and capacity expands.

diagnostic_logic: Populates the Moat Taxonomy panel in the synthesis output. Multiple or network-effect moats receive the highest durability scores.

question_text: Using Greenwald's framework, what is the primary source of competitive advantage?

options.label: Customer captivity — habit, switching costs, or search costs keep customers
Production advantage — proprietary technology or access to cheap resources
Local scale — dominant in a geographic or niche market
None — the company competes in an open, contestable market

why_this_matters: Bruce Greenwald's 'Competition Demystified' argues that competitive advantages are rarer than most investors believe, and they come from only three sources: customer captivity (buyers stay because leaving is costly), production advantages (the company can make things competitors cannot), or economies of scale in a defined market. If none of these apply, the company does not have a sustainable competitive advantage — regardless of its current profitability.

worked_examples.situation: Dominant in local markets where it has route density advantages. A competitor would need to match route density in each local market to match costs — prohibitively expensive.
worked_examples.outcome: Local scale economy gives Waste Management 30%+ gross margins in its core markets — a result that looks impossible for a 'garbage company' until you understand route density economics.

worked_examples.situation: No structural customer captivity (easy to cancel), no production advantage (anyone can make shows), no local scale. Competes in an open market for subscribers.
worked_examples.outcome: Greenwald's framework explains why Netflix's margins are under pressure despite massive scale — the company lacks a structural source of advantage.

diagnostic_logic: Cross-validates the Dorsey moat identification. Alignment between Dorsey and Greenwald increases confidence; divergence flags potential misidentification.

question_text: How does the product's functional job interact with its emotional/social job?

options.label: Stated functional job only — product hired for utility
Emotional or social job — product hired for feeling or identity
Both, mutually reinforcing — function validates the emotional choice
Job is being redefined by substitutes or market shifts

why_this_matters: Christensen's insight is that customers do not buy products — they hire products to do jobs. The most resilient businesses serve both a functional and an emotional/social job simultaneously, where each reinforces the other. A Patagonia jacket keeps you warm (functional) AND signals environmental values (social). When both jobs align, substitution becomes nearly impossible because a competitor would need to replicate both dimensions.

worked_examples.situation: Sells outdoor gear (functional: warmth, durability) that simultaneously signals environmental consciousness and outdoor identity (social/emotional).
worked_examples.outcome: Mutually reinforcing jobs allow Patagonia to maintain 50%+ gross margins in a category (outdoor apparel) where comparable products exist at 1/3 the price.

worked_examples.situation: The job was being redefined: smartphones shifted from 'email device for executives' (BlackBerry's job) to 'personal computing platform' (iPhone/Android's job).
worked_examples.outcome: When the job itself changes, even a dominant incumbent loses relevance. BlackBerry's market share went from 43% to 0.1% in five years.

diagnostic_logic: Reinforcing jobs generate the highest substitution resistance. Job redefinition is an urgent fragility signal.

question_text: Can this company raise prices at or above inflation without losing volume?

options.label: Demonstrated yes — multi-decade track record of real price increases
Probably yes — no clear disconfirmation, but untested in adversity
Demonstrated no — prices erode in real terms over time

why_this_matters: Warren Buffett has called pricing power 'the single most important decision in evaluating a business.' His concept of economic goodwill — the ability to earn returns above the cost of tangible assets — flows directly from the power to raise prices. See's Candies, Buffett's archetype, raises prices every year with no volume loss because the product is hired for a social job (gift-giving) where price itself signals quality and care.

worked_examples.situation: Has raised prices every year for 50+ years, consistently above inflation. Volume has remained stable or grown. No customer has switched to a cheaper chocolate because the gift-giving job requires perceived premium quality.
worked_examples.outcome: See's Candies returns 60%+ on invested capital. Buffett has called it the ideal business: high returns on low tangible capital, funded entirely by pricing power.

worked_examples.situation: Average selling prices have declined in real terms for 30 years. HP, Dell, and Lenovo face continuous price deflation driven by component commoditization and buyer power.
worked_examples.outcome: Real price erosion compresses margins over time regardless of cost-cutting efficiency. PC makers earn 15-20% gross margins despite massive scale.

diagnostic_logic: This is the single most powerful diagnostic signal. Demonstrated pricing power is the strongest predictor of durable gross margins.
